summary: The author examines whether demographic trends might reduce college tuition costs by the time his daughters reach college age. He notes that while college enrollment peaked around 2010 and the pool of college-age students is shrinking by about 8%, elite universities have insulated themselves by recruiting wealthy foreign students. Despite factors that should pressure tuition downward—declining enrollment, flat wages, student loan concerns, and online education—he remains skeptical that costs will actually fall. Drawing on his experience watching the housing bubble, he concludes that bubbles are unpredictable and the only rational response is to keep saving rather than betting on a collapse.
key_points: College enrollment peaked in 2010 and has been declining, with about 1.7 million fewer kids in the pipeline compared to current college-age students

Elite universities have protected themselves from demographic pressure by aggressively recruiting wealthy foreign students, becoming what Brad DeLong called 'finishing schools for the superrich of Asia'
Moody's reports that 40% of rated colleges face stagnant or declining net tuition revenue, with some smaller schools entering a 'death spiral'
A Harvard Business School professor projects that half of the 4,000 US colleges may fail in the next 15 years
The author's experience with the housing bubble taught him that bubbles last longer than expected and their bursting is impossible to predict
money_quotes: It's an odd sort of competition, though, where schools are competing for a particular customer (wealthy students) by raising prices.
His school, Berkeley, and other top universities presumably had become 'finishing schools for the superrich of Asia.'

As someone who watched, first hand and with great discomfort, the growth of the subprime and housing bubbles before the crisis, I've painfully learned that bubbles can last much longer than you would rationally expect.
Despite all sorts of analysis and calculation about what should happen, the thing that triggers the bursting of the bubble is really hard to predict.
You shouldn't do anything stupid in anticipation of the bubble either bursting or continuing.
Both college tuition and student loans have been described as bubbles thanks to their recent rate of growth. At some point, bubbles burst (in theory).
key_insight: Despite demographic and economic factors that should logically reduce college tuition, the unpredictable nature of bubbles means the only rational strategy is to keep saving rather than betting on market correction.
